title: Berkshire Hathaway’s Pay Structure Is Straight Out of the ‘60s. Why That’s a Good Thing.
summary: When it comes to corporate governance,  Berkshire Hathaway  is a throwback to the 1960s and unique among major U.S. companies.  It showed that virtually nothing in the how the company is run would change under CEO Greg Abel, who succeeded Warren Buffett at the start of 2026.  Berkshire doesn’t issue any stock compensation to executives—or any employee for that matter—and goes as far as stating it “never intends” to do so.

title: Chevron’s Libya Expansion And Iran War Shock Test Valuation And Risks
summary: Chevron (NYSE:CVX) has secured a new exploration block in Libya, expanding into a high risk, resource rich market. Chevron is reshaping its global portfolio, including asset sales and restructuring, during a period of record oil prices. summary: Value is of the utmost importance to Greg Abel and Berkshire Hathaway's now-retired CEO, Warren Buffett.

title: Buffett Hands Reins To Abel As Berkshire Revives Buybacks And Ownership Focus
summary: Warren Buffett has retired as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) after roughly six decades at the company. Greg Abel has taken over as CEO, marking a formal handover of day to day leadership. Abel has made a significant personal purchase of Berkshire shares, aligning his financial stake more closely with shareholders. Berkshire has resumed share repurchases after a pause, signaling a renewed use of buybacks in its capital allocation toolkit. Berkshire Hathaway operates across multiple...

title: Greg Abel Signals Berkshire Confidence With Personal Buy And Buybacks
summary: New CEO Greg Abel purchased US$15 million in NYSE:BRK.B stock, signaling personal commitment to the company. Berkshire Hathaway resumed share buybacks for the first time in nearly two years under Abel's leadership. These are among the first major capital allocation moves since Warren Buffett's retirement. title: Berkshire Could Repurchase Over $50 Billion of Stock Annually Based on Recent Buy
summary: Berkshire Hathaway  probably is capable of repurchasing over $50 billion of stock annually if it chooses to be aggressive with stock buybacks.  An annual buyback of $50 billion-plus is certainly doable given the company’s earnings power of about $45 billion annually and its huge cash reserves of $373 billion.  Many Berkshire holders would like to see the company get more aggressive with stock repurchases because they view the shares as undervalued and would like the company to draw down its cash reserves.
